articles.headline: Centre Launches 'Gram Uday' Scheme for Sustainable Rural Transformation
articles.explainer:
• The Union Government today unveiled the 'Gram Uday' scheme, a comprehensive initiative aimed at holistic rural development across the nation.
• The scheme integrates various existing programs under a single umbrella, focusing on sustainable livelihoods, climate resilience, and infrastructure development.
• Key pillars include promoting organic farming, water conservation, renewable energy adoption in villages, and enhancing digital literacy.
• Emphasizes community participation and Gram Panchayat empowerment for localized planning and implementation.
• Aims to bridge the rural-urban divide by improving access to education, healthcare, and economic opportunities in rural areas.
gsSubTopics: Government Policies and Interventions for Development in various sectors and Issues arising out of their Design and Implementation
Welfare Schemes for Vulnerable Sections of the population by the Centre and States and the Performance of these Schemes
Role of NGOs, SHGs, various groups and associations, donors, charities, institutional and other stakeholders
prelimsPoints: 'Gram Uday' scheme focuses on sustainable livelihoods, climate resilience, and infrastructure.
It integrates existing rural development programs.
Emphasizes Gram Panchayat empowerment and community participation.
Targets include organic farming, water conservation, renewable energy, and digital literacy in rural areas.
mainsPoints: Discuss the multi-faceted approach of Gram Uday in addressing rural challenges.
Evaluate the potential impact of integrating various schemes under one umbrella for better synergy and resource utilization.
Analyze the role of decentralized planning and community participation in achieving sustainable rural transformation.
Examine how the scheme aims to address climate resilience and promote sustainable agricultural practices.
articles.imageDescription: Illustration depicting a vibrant Indian village scene with elements of renewable energy, organic farming, and digital literacy.
articles.trivia: Mahatma Gandhi's vision of 'Gram Swaraj' emphasized self-sufficient and self-governing villages, a principle often echoed in India's rural development policies.

articles.headline: Supreme Court to Review Compliance on Electoral Bonds Transparency
articles.explainer:
• The Supreme Court has indicated it will review the compliance status of its earlier judgment regarding the disclosure of electoral bond details.
• This move follows petitions alleging incomplete or delayed disclosure by certain entities and political parties.
• The Court had previously struck down the Electoral Bond Scheme as unconstitutional, citing violations of the right to information and voter's right to know.
• The review aims to ensure full transparency in political funding ahead of upcoming state elections.
• The judgment has been a landmark decision in promoting financial transparency in Indian politics.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure.
Separation of powers between various organs dispute redressal mechanisms and institutions.
Salient features of the Representation of People’s Act.
prelimsPoints: Supreme Court reviewing compliance on electoral bond disclosure.
Court previously struck down the scheme as unconstitutional.
Cited violation of right to information and voter's right to know.
Aims to ensure full transparency in political funding.
mainsPoints: Discuss the implications of the Supreme Court's ruling on electoral bonds for democratic transparency and accountability.
Analyze the arguments for and against electoral bonds in the context of political funding reforms.
Evaluate the role of the judiciary in upholding constitutional principles related to fundamental rights like the right to information.
Suggest alternative mechanisms for transparent political funding in India.
articles.imageDescription: Image of the Supreme Court of India building.
articles.trivia: The first general elections in India in 1951-52 saw the use of ballot boxes, a system that has evolved significantly over decades to electronic voting machines.

articles.headline: India Reiterates Call for Enhanced Climate Finance at COP29 Preparatory Talks
articles.explainer:
• India's delegation at the preparatory talks for COP29 emphasized the critical need for developed nations to meet their climate finance commitments.
• Stressed the importance of predictable and adequate financial support for developing countries to achieve their climate mitigation and adaptation goals.
• Highlighted the principle of 'Common but Differentiated Responsibilities and Respective Capabilities' (CBDR-RC) as foundational to global climate action.
• Called for a clear roadmap for achieving the $100 billion annual climate finance goal and scaling up beyond it post-2025.
• India presented its progress in renewable energy deployment and sustainable development initiatives, urging global collaboration.
gsSubTopics: Environmental pollution and degradation
Conservation
Environmental impact assessment
India and its neighbourhood- relations.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
prelimsPoints: India emphasized enhanced climate finance at COP29 preparatory talks.
Called for predictable and adequate financial support for developing nations.
Reiterated CBDR-RC principle.
Demanded clear roadmap for $100 billion climate finance goal and beyond.
mainsPoints: Discuss India's consistent stance on climate finance at international forums and its rationale.
Analyze the concept of 'Common but Differentiated Responsibilities and Respective Capabilities' (CBDR-RC) in the context of global climate justice.
Evaluate the challenges in achieving climate finance goals and the implications for developing countries like India.
Propose strategies for effective global collaboration to address climate change, considering equity and historical responsibility.
articles.imageDescription: Delegates from various countries at a climate conference.
articles.trivia: The first Earth Summit in Rio de Janeiro in 1992 led to the establishment of the UNFCCC, the framework for international climate action.

articles.headline: Air Quality Deteriorates in Northern Indian Cities; Government Urges Stricter Enforcement
articles.explainer:
• A recent report indicates a significant deterioration in air quality across several northern Indian cities, including Delhi-NCR, with AQI levels entering the 'very poor' category.
• Factors contributing to the worsening air quality include stubble burning, vehicular emissions, industrial pollution, and unfavorable meteorological conditions.
• The government has urged state authorities to implement stricter measures under the Graded Response Action Plan (GRAP) and ensure public compliance.
• Health advisories have been issued, particularly for vulnerable populations, recommending reduced outdoor activities.
• Long-term strategies focusing on renewable energy, public transport, and industrial emission controls are being re-evaluated for faster implementation.
gsSubTopics: Environmental pollution and degradation
Conservation
Environmental impact assessment
prelimsPoints: Air quality in northern Indian cities worsened to 'very poor' category.
Causes include stubble burning, vehicular emissions, industrial pollution.
Government urged stricter GRAP implementation.
Health advisories issued for vulnerable groups.
mainsPoints: Analyze the multi-faceted causes of air pollution in northern India and its seasonal variations.
Critically evaluate the effectiveness of existing policies and action plans like GRAP in mitigating air pollution.
Discuss the socio-economic and health impacts of deteriorating air quality on urban populations.
Suggest comprehensive long-term and short-term strategies to effectively combat air pollution in India.
articles.imageDescription: Smog-filled skyline of a major Indian city.
articles.trivia: The Great Smog of London in 1952 led to significant changes in environmental regulations and cleaner air acts in the UK.

articles.headline: India and Japan Conclude 'JIMEX 24' Naval Exercise, Bolstering Maritime Security Cooperation
articles.explainer:
• The bilateral maritime exercise 'JIMEX 24' between the Indian Navy and the Japan Maritime Self-Defense Force (JMSDF) concluded successfully in the Arabian Sea.
• The exercise focused on enhancing interoperability, sharing best practices, and strengthening maritime security cooperation in the Indo-Pacific region.
• Drills included anti-submarine warfare, anti-air warfare, tactical maneuvers, and communication exercises.
• JIMEX (Japan India Maritime Exercise) is a biennial exercise reflecting the growing strategic partnership between the two nations.
• The collaboration underscores a shared vision for a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific, crucial for regional stability and economic prosperity.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighbourhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
prelimsPoints: JIMEX 24 is a bilateral naval exercise between India and Japan.
Concluded in the Arabian Sea.
Aimed at enhancing interoperability and maritime security in the Indo-Pacific.
Focuses on anti-submarine, anti-air warfare, and tactical maneuvers.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic significance of India-Japan defense cooperation in the context of Indo-Pacific security dynamics.
Analyze how joint naval exercises like JIMEX contribute to regional stability and counterbalancing emerging challenges.
Evaluate the role of bilateral and multilateral security partnerships in India's foreign policy objectives.
Examine the shared vision of India and Japan for a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific and its implications.
articles.imageDescription: Warships of the Indian and Japanese navies conducting maneuvers at sea.
articles.trivia: The Indian Navy's oldest operational aircraft carrier, INS Vikrant (R11), served for over 36 years before being decommissioned.

articles.headline: ISRO Successfully Launches Next-Gen Weather Satellite 'Kalpana-3'
articles.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) successfully launched 'Kalpana-3', an advanced geostationary meteorological satellite, from Satish Dhawan Space Centre.
• Kalpana-3 is designed to provide high-resolution images of weather patterns, monitor ocean surface temperatures, and track atmospheric phenomena.
• Equipped with advanced payloads including a multi-spectral imager and a sounder, it will significantly enhance India's weather forecasting capabilities.
• The satellite is a successor to the Kalpana series, named in honor of astronaut Kalpana Chawla, and will bolster disaster warning systems.
• This mission reinforces India's growing self-reliance and leadership in space technology for societal applications.
gsSubTopics: Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Awareness in the fields of Space.
Disaster and disaster management.
prelimsPoints: ISRO launched 'Kalpana-3', a next-gen geostationary meteorological satellite.
Designed for high-resolution weather imaging, ocean temperature monitoring, atmospheric tracking.
Equipped with multi-spectral imager and sounder.
Successor to Kalpana series, named after Kalpana Chawla.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of advanced meteorological satellites like Kalpana-3 for India's weather forecasting and disaster management capabilities.
Analyze ISRO's contributions to societal applications through its space missions, citing relevant examples.
Evaluate India's growing self-reliance and leadership in space technology and its implications for national development.
Examine the role of space-based observations in understanding and mitigating the impacts of climate change.
articles.imageDescription: Rocket launching into space with 'Kalpana-3' satellite.
articles.trivia: Kalpana Chawla was the first woman of Indian origin to go to space, flying on the Space Shuttle Columbia in 1997.

articles.headline: Government Unveils New Incentives to Boost MSME Exports and Global Competitiveness
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Micro, Small & Medium Enterprises (MSME) announced a new package of incentives and support mechanisms to significantly boost MSME exports.
• Measures include enhanced credit access for export-oriented units, simplified customs procedures, and subsidies for international trade fair participation.
• Focus is on developing specialized export clusters and promoting 'Made in India' brands in global markets.
• A new digital portal has been launched to streamline export documentation and provide market intelligence to MSMEs.
• Aims to integrate Indian MSMEs more deeply into global value chains and achieve higher export growth targets.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization, of resources, growth, development and employment.
Inclusive growth and issues arising from it.
Effects of liberalization on the economy, changes in industrial policy and their effects on industrial growth.
prelimsPoints: Ministry of MSME launched new incentives for MSME exports.
Measures include enhanced credit, simplified customs, trade fair subsidies.
Focus on export clusters and 'Made in India' brands.
New digital portal for export documentation and market intelligence.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of MSMEs in India's economic growth and employment generation.
Analyze the challenges faced by Indian MSMEs in accessing global markets and competing internationally.
Evaluate the effectiveness of government initiatives aimed at boosting MSME exports and integrating them into global value chains.
Suggest further policy interventions to enhance the global competitiveness of India's MSME sector.
articles.imageDescription: MSME products being prepared for export with a globe in the background.
articles.trivia: India's MSME sector contributes significantly to the country's GDP and employment, often referred to as the 'backbone of the economy'.

articles.headline: Mid-Term Review of National Health Policy 2017 Shows Mixed Progress, Focus on Primary Healthcare
articles.explainer:
• A mid-term review of the National Health Policy 2017 revealed mixed progress in achieving its ambitious targets, particularly in public health spending.
• Significant strides have been made in expanding Ayushman Bharat-PMJAY coverage and improving maternal and child health indicators.
• Challenges persist in strengthening primary healthcare infrastructure, addressing human resource shortages, and ensuring equitable access in rural areas.
• The review highlighted the need for increased state government allocation to health and better coordination between central and state health programs.
• Emphasizes leveraging digital health technologies and promoting preventive healthcare for better outcomes.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
Welfare Schemes for Vulnerable Sections of the population by the Centre and States and the Performance of these Schemes.
articles.source: Ministry of Health and Family Welfare
prelimsPoints: Mid-term review of National Health Policy 2017 shows mixed progress.
Progress in Ayushman Bharat-PMJAY and maternal/child health.
Challenges in primary healthcare infrastructure and human resources.
Need for increased state health allocation and better center-state coordination.
mainsPoints: Critically analyze the achievements and shortcomings of the National Health Policy 2017 in improving India's public health outcomes.
Discuss the challenges in achieving universal healthcare coverage and equitable access, especially in rural and remote areas.
Evaluate the role of flagship schemes like Ayushman Bharat-PMJAY in transforming India's healthcare landscape.
Suggest policy recommendations to strengthen primary healthcare and address human resource shortages in the health sector.
articles.imageDescription: A doctor interacting with patients in a rural health center.
articles.trivia: The 'Janani Shishu Suraksha Karyakram' (JSSK) was launched in 2011 to reduce maternal and infant mortality by providing free services to pregnant women and sick newborns.

articles.headline: Archaeologists Unearth New Evidence of Pre-Harappan Settlement in Gujarat
articles.explainer:
• A team of archaeologists has announced the discovery of significant artifacts and structural remains suggesting a large pre-Harappan settlement in Gujarat.
• The findings include pottery, tools, and remnants of planned structures, pushing back the known timeline of organized human habitation in the region.
• This discovery sheds new light on the evolution of the Indus Valley Civilization and its precursor cultures.
• Further excavations are expected to reveal more about the socio-economic and cultural practices of these early inhabitants.
• The site's strategic location could indicate its role in early trade networks.
gsSubTopics: Indian Heritage and Culture, History and Geography of the World and Society.
Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.
prelimsPoints: New pre-Harappan settlement discovered in Gujarat.
Findings include pottery, tools, planned structures.
Sheds light on evolution of Indus Valley Civilization.
Location suggests role in early trade networks.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of new archaeological discoveries in reshaping our understanding of ancient Indian history and the Indus Valley Civilization.
Analyze the characteristics of pre-Harappan cultures and their contribution to the development of urban civilizations in the subcontinent.
Evaluate the methods and challenges involved in archaeological research in India.
Examine the potential implications of such discoveries for understanding trade, social structures, and cultural exchanges in ancient India.
articles.imageDescription: Archaeologists at an excavation site, carefully unearthing artifacts.
articles.trivia: The Indus Valley Civilization is one of the world's three early cradles of civilization, along with ancient Egypt and Mesopotamia.

articles.headline: Urban Local Bodies Directed to Enhance Monsoon Preparedness Against Flooding
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs has issued directives to urban local bodies (ULBs) across the country to intensify monsoon preparedness measures.
• The focus is on clearing drainage systems, identifying flood-prone areas, upgrading storm water infrastructure, and developing early warning systems.
• Emphasizes community engagement and public awareness campaigns on flood safety and waste management to prevent blockages.
• The directives come in light of increasing instances of urban flooding due to erratic rainfall patterns and rapid urbanization.
• Calls for integrating climate-resilient urban planning into long-term development strategies.
gsSubTopics: Important Geophysical phenomena such as earthquakes, Tsunami, Volcanic activity, cyclone etc., geographical features and their location-changes in critical geographical features (including water-bodies and ice-caps) and in flora and fauna and the effects of such changes.
Urbanization, their problems and their remedies.
articles.source: Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs
prelimsPoints: Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs directed ULBs for monsoon preparedness.
Focus on clearing drainage, identifying flood-prone areas, upgrading storm water infrastructure.
Emphasizes community engagement and public awareness.
Calls for integrating climate-resilient urban planning.
mainsPoints: Analyze the primary causes and increasing frequency of urban flooding in Indian cities.
Critically evaluate the existing disaster management frameworks and urban planning strategies in addressing urban flooding.
Discuss the socio-economic impacts of urban flooding on city dwellers and infrastructure.
Suggest comprehensive measures, including structural and non-structural interventions, for effective urban flood management and climate-resilient urban development.
articles.imageDescription: A waterlogged street in an Indian city during heavy monsoon rain.
articles.trivia: Mumbai's Mithi River, once a clean river, is now heavily polluted and prone to overflowing, contributing to the city's urban flooding challenges.

articles.headline: RBI Keeps Repo Rate Unchanged Amidst Persistent Inflationary Concerns
articles.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) in its latest Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) meeting decided to keep the benchmark repo rate unchanged at 6.5%.
• The decision reflects a cautious stance, prioritizing inflation control despite signs of moderating economic growth.
• RBI Governor highlighted concerns over volatile food prices and global crude oil fluctuations as key inflationary risks.
• The MPC reiterated its commitment to aligning inflation with the target while supporting growth.
• Future policy decisions will remain data-dependent, closely monitoring domestic and international economic indicators.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization, of resources, growth, development and employment.
Government Budgeting.
Monetary policy.
prelimsPoints: RBI kept repo rate unchanged at 6.5%.
Decision driven by persistent inflationary concerns.
Volatile food prices and crude oil cited as risks.
MPC committed to inflation target and growth support.
mainsPoints: Discuss the primary objectives of the RBI's monetary policy and the tools it employs to achieve them.
Analyze the current economic challenges facing India, particularly the trade-off between inflation control and economic growth.
Evaluate the rationale behind the RBI's decision to maintain the repo rate and its potential impact on various sectors of the economy.
Examine the role of the Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) in India's economic governance and its independence.
articles.imageDescription: RBI building facade with the logo.
articles.trivia: The Reserve Bank of India was established on April 1, 1935, in accordance with the Reserve Bank of India Act, 1934.

articles.headline: India Unveils Enhanced Cybersecurity Framework to Combat Rising Digital Threats
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) has released an enhanced National Cybersecurity Framework, updating existing policies.
• The new framework focuses on critical infrastructure protection, promoting indigenous cybersecurity solutions, and fostering international cooperation.
• Introduces stricter regulations for data protection and incident response protocols for government agencies and private entities.
• Aims to build a robust cyber defense ecosystem, including skill development and public awareness campaigns against cybercrime.
• This move comes in response to the growing sophistication of cyberattacks targeting both public and private sectors.
gsSubTopics: Challenges to internal security through communication networks, role of media and social networking sites in internal security challenges, basics of cyber security; money-laundering and its prevention.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
articles.source: Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY)
prelimsPoints: MeitY unveiled enhanced National Cybersecurity Framework.
Focuses on critical infrastructure protection, indigenous solutions, international cooperation.
Stricter regulations for data protection and incident response.
Aims to build robust cyber defense ecosystem and skill development.
mainsPoints: Discuss the evolving landscape of cyber threats faced by India and their potential impact on national security and economic stability.
Analyze the key provisions and objectives of India's enhanced National Cybersecurity Framework.
Evaluate the challenges in implementing a comprehensive cybersecurity strategy, including technological, human resource, and legal aspects.
Suggest measures for fostering a culture of cybersecurity awareness and promoting indigenous capabilities in this domain.
articles.imageDescription: Abstract image representing digital security and a network grid.
articles.trivia: The first computer virus, 'Creeper', was created in 1971, while the first known cyberattack on critical infrastructure was the Stuxnet worm in 2010.

articles.headline: India Accelerates Renewable Energy Deployment to Meet Ambitious 2030 Targets
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy announced accelerated plans to deploy renewable energy projects, aiming to surpass the 500 GW target by 2030.
• Focus includes large-scale solar parks, offshore wind farms, and green hydrogen initiatives.
• New policies offer incentives for private sector investment and technological innovation in the renewable energy sector.
• Emphasizes the role of renewable energy in achieving energy security, reducing carbon emissions, and fostering green jobs.
• International collaborations and technology transfer are being actively pursued to scale up deployment.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
articles.source: Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE)
prelimsPoints: MNRE announced accelerated renewable energy deployment plans.
Aims to surpass 500 GW target by 2030.
Focus on solar parks, offshore wind, green hydrogen.
New policies for private investment and tech innovation.
mainsPoints: Discuss India's ambitious renewable energy targets and their significance for climate action and energy security.
Analyze the challenges and opportunities in scaling up renewable energy deployment in India, including land acquisition, grid integration, and financing.
Evaluate the role of government policies and private sector investment in driving India's energy transition.
Examine the potential of green hydrogen as a future energy source for India and the steps being taken for its development.
articles.imageDescription: A vast solar power farm stretching into the horizon.
articles.trivia: India is home to the world's largest solar power plant, Bhadla Solar Park in Rajasthan, with a capacity of over 2.2 GW.

articles.headline: Experts Discuss Implementation Challenges of National Education Policy 2020
articles.explainer:
• A national conference of educationists and policymakers discussed the ongoing challenges in the effective implementation of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020.
• Key issues highlighted include infrastructure gaps, teacher training and recruitment, curriculum development for multidisciplinary learning, and funding constraints.
• Emphasis was laid on the need for greater collaboration between central and state governments for seamless policy rollout.
• The NEP 2020 aims to transform India's education system from early childhood to higher education, focusing on holistic development and skill building.
• Stakeholders called for a phased and adaptive approach, prioritizing foundational literacy and numeracy.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
Government Policies and Interventions for Development in various sectors and Issues arising out of their Design and Implementation.
prelimsPoints: Conference discussed NEP 2020 implementation challenges.
Issues: infrastructure, teacher training, curriculum, funding.
Need for center-state collaboration for rollout.
NEP aims for holistic development and skill building.
mainsPoints: Analyze the transformative potential of the National Education Policy 2020 in reforming India's education system.
Discuss the major implementation challenges faced by the NEP 2020, particularly concerning resources, human capital, and federal cooperation.
Evaluate the importance of foundational literacy and numeracy, and multidisciplinary education as envisioned by the NEP.
Suggest strategies for effective and equitable implementation of the NEP across diverse educational landscapes in India.
articles.imageDescription: Students engaged in learning activities in a modern classroom setting.
articles.trivia: India's first National Policy on Education was formulated in 1968, followed by significant revisions in 1986 and 1992.

articles.headline: India-Africa Business Summit Concludes, Pledges Enhanced Trade and Investment
articles.explainer:
• The India-Africa Business Summit concluded in New Delhi, bringing together business leaders and policymakers from both regions.
• The summit focused on enhancing bilateral trade, fostering investment, and promoting technology transfer in key sectors like agriculture, healthcare, and digital economy.
• India reiterated its commitment to capacity building and development partnerships with African nations, aligning with the 'SAGAR' vision.
• Several Memoranda of Understanding (MoUs) were signed to facilitate joint ventures and infrastructure projects.
• The event underscored the growing strategic importance of India-Africa relations in a multipolar world.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighbourhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
prelimsPoints: India-Africa Business Summit concluded in New Delhi.
Focused on enhancing bilateral trade, investment, technology transfer.
India committed to capacity building and development partnerships.
Several MoUs signed for joint ventures and infrastructure projects.
mainsPoints: Discuss the evolving dynamics and strategic significance of India-Africa relations in the 21st century.
Analyze the key pillars of India's engagement with African nations, including trade, investment, and development cooperation.
Evaluate the challenges and opportunities in strengthening economic ties between India and Africa.
Examine how India's 'SAGAR' vision and South-South cooperation principles guide its foreign policy towards Africa.
articles.imageDescription: Business leaders from India and African nations shaking hands at a conference.
articles.trivia: The 'Bandung Conference' in 1955 was a significant milestone in Afro-Asian solidarity, laying the groundwork for the Non-Aligned Movement.

articles.headline: Several Traditional Indian Crafts Awarded GI Tag, Boosting Artisanal Livelihoods
articles.explainer:
• The Geographical Indications (GI) Registry has recently granted GI tags to several traditional Indian crafts from different states.
• This recognition helps protect the unique identity and quality of these indigenous products, preventing their unauthorized use.
• The move is expected to boost the livelihoods of local artisans, promote traditional knowledge, and enhance the market value of these crafts.
• Examples include a unique form of pottery from Rajasthan, handloom textiles from the North East, and a specific wood carving style from South India.
• The government aims to further promote these GI-tagged products through national and international exhibitions.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.
Salient features of Indian Society, Diversity of India.
articles.source: Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT)
prelimsPoints: Several traditional Indian crafts received GI tags.
Protects unique identity and quality of indigenous products.
Aims to boost artisans' livelihoods and promote traditional knowledge.
Government plans to promote GI-tagged products nationally and internationally.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of Geographical Indication (GI) tags in preserving India's cultural heritage and promoting local economies.
Analyze how GI tags contribute to protecting traditional knowledge and preventing imitation of indigenous products.
Evaluate the challenges faced by traditional artisans in India and how policy interventions like GI tagging can address them.
Suggest further measures to enhance the market access and global recognition of India's diverse traditional crafts.
articles.imageDescription: A collection of colorful traditional Indian handicrafts.
articles.trivia: Darjeeling Tea was the first Indian product to receive a Geographical Indication (GI) tag in 2004-05.

highlights: Centre launches 'Gram Uday' scheme for sustainable rural transformation, integrating various development programs.
Supreme Court to review compliance on electoral bonds transparency, ensuring full disclosure ahead of state elections.
India reiterates call for enhanced climate finance at COP29 preparatory talks, emphasizing CBDR-RC principle.
ISRO successfully launches 'Kalpana-3', a next-gen weather satellite, bolstering India's meteorological capabilities.
RBI keeps benchmark repo rate unchanged at 6.5% amidst persistent inflationary concerns, prioritizing price stability.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to the 'Gram Uday' scheme, consider the following statements:
1. It is a comprehensive initiative focused on urban development and smart city projects.
2. The scheme aims to promote organic farming and renewable energy adoption in villages.
3. It emphasizes community participation and Gram Panchayat empowerment for localized planning.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is incorrect. The 'Gram Uday' scheme is aimed at holistic *rural* development, not urban development or smart city projects. Statements 2 and 3 are correct, as the scheme focuses on sustainable livelihoods, climate resilience, including organic farming, renewable energy, and emphasizes community participation and Gram Panchayat empowerment.

prelimsMocks.question: Which of the following principles is India consistently advocating for at international climate change forums regarding climate finance?
options: (b) Common but Differentiated Responsibilities and Respective Capabilities (CBDR-RC)
(c) Net Zero Emissions by All Nations
prelimsMocks.answer: (b) Common but Differentiated Responsibilities and Respective Capabilities (CBDR-RC)
prelimsMocks.explanation: India consistently advocates for the principle of 'Common but Differentiated Responsibilities and Respective Capabilities' (CBDR-RC) at international climate change forums, stressing that developed nations have a greater historical responsibility and capacity to provide climate finance to developing countries.

prelimsMocks.question:
Consider the following statements regarding the 'Kalpana-3' satellite recently launched by ISRO:
1. It is a Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellite for Earth observation.
2. Its primary function is to provide high-resolution images of weather patterns and monitor ocean surface temperatures.
3. It is named in honor of Dr. A.P.J. Abdul Kalam.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: Statement 1 is incorrect; Kalpana-3 is an advanced *geostationary* meteorological satellite, not a LEO satellite. Statement 2 is correct, as its design focuses on weather patterns and ocean temperature monitoring. Statement 3 is incorrect; it is named in honor of astronaut Kalpana Chawla, not Dr. A.P.J. Abdul Kalam.

prelimsMocks.question:
Which of the following factors are primary contributors to the deterioration of air quality in northern Indian cities?
1. Stubble burning
2. Vehicular emissions
3. Industrial pollution
4. Favorable meteorological conditions
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
prelimsMocks.explanation: Stubble burning, vehicular emissions, and industrial pollution are major contributors to air quality deterioration. Favorable meteorological conditions (like strong winds) typically help disperse pollutants, while *unfavorable* conditions (like low wind speed and temperature inversion) exacerbate the problem. Hence, statement 4 is incorrect.

prelimsMocks.question: With respect to the Reserve Bank of India's (RBI) Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) decisions, which of the following is a primary objective of keeping the repo rate unchanged amidst inflationary concerns?
options: (a) To boost credit growth for rapid economic expansion.
(b) To prioritize inflation control and ensure price stability.
(c) To encourage foreign direct investment by making borrowing cheaper.
(d) To devalue the currency to make exports more competitive.
prelimsMocks.answer: (b) To prioritize inflation control and ensure price stability.
prelimsMocks.explanation: The RBI's primary mandate is price stability, and keeping the repo rate unchanged (or raising it) during inflationary concerns is a measure to control inflation by making borrowing more expensive, thereby reducing money supply and demand.

mainsMocks.question: Critically analyze the efficacy of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 in addressing the persistent challenges of access, equity, and quality in India's education system. Discuss the key implementation hurdles and suggest measures for a more inclusive and effective rollout. (15 marks, 250 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Education, Human Resources.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce NEP 2020's vision. Analyze its potential to address access (ECCE, universal schooling), equity (inclusion, disadvantaged groups), and quality (holistic, skill-based learning). Discuss implementation hurdles like funding, infrastructure, teacher training, center-state coordination, and digital divide. Conclude with suggestions for phased, adaptive, and resource-backed implementation.

mainsMocks.question: India's commitment to renewable energy targets is crucial for both climate action and energy security. Discuss the strategies adopted by India to accelerate renewable energy deployment and evaluate the associated challenges, including land acquisition, grid integration, and financing. (15 marks, 250 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment. Infrastructure: Energy.
mainsMocks.approach: Start with India's ambitious RE targets (e.g., 500GW by 2030) and their dual importance. Detail strategies like solar parks, offshore wind, green hydrogen, policy incentives, and international collaborations. Then, critically examine challenges: land availability, grid stability/integration, high initial capital costs, financing mechanisms, and technological advancements. Conclude with a balanced outlook on overcoming these hurdles.

mainsMocks.question: The Supreme Court's stance on electoral bonds has significantly impacted financial transparency in Indian politics. Discuss the constitutional implications of the Electoral Bond Scheme and the judiciary's role in upholding the 'right to know' of voters. Suggest reforms for transparent political funding in India. (10 marks, 150 words)
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Indian Constitution—significant provisions and basic structure. Separation of powers between various organs dispute redressal mechanisms and institutions. Salient features of the Representation of People’s Act.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce the SC's ruling on electoral bonds. Discuss constitutional implications, particularly violation of Article 19(1)(a) (right to information) and voter's right to know. Explain the judiciary's role as a guardian of fundamental rights. Suggest reforms like a National Electoral Fund, direct corporate donations with full disclosure, state funding of elections, and strengthening election commission powers.

quickBytes.text: The 'Gram Uday' scheme aims for holistic rural development, focusing on sustainable livelihoods and climate resilience.
The Supreme Court recently reviewed compliance on electoral bonds, emphasizing voter's right to know and transparency.
India advocates 'Common but Differentiated Responsibilities and Respective Capabilities' (CBDR-RC) for climate finance.
ISRO launched 'Kalpana-3', an advanced geostationary meteorological satellite for enhanced weather forecasting.
The RBI Monetary Policy Committee kept the repo rate unchanged at 6.5% to prioritize inflation control.
JIMEX 24 is a bilateral maritime exercise between the Indian Navy and Japan Maritime Self-Defense Force.
A new pre-Harappan settlement discovery in Gujarat sheds light on early Indus Valley Civilization precursors.
India's enhanced National Cybersecurity Framework aims to protect critical infrastructure from digital threats.
The National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 aims for holistic development and skill building in education.
Geographical Indication (GI) tags help protect unique traditional crafts and boost artisanal livelihoods.

GS1.headline: Archaeologists Unearth New Evidence of Pre-Harappan Settlement in Gujarat
GS1.explainer:
• A team of archaeologists has announced the discovery of significant artifacts and structural remains suggesting a large pre-Harappan settlement in Gujarat.
• The findings include pottery, tools, and remnants of planned structures, pushing back the known timeline of organized human habitation in the region.
• This discovery sheds new light on the evolution of the Indus Valley Civilization and its precursor cultures.
• Further excavations are expected to reveal more about the socio-economic and cultural practices of these early inhabitants.
• The site's strategic location could indicate its role in early trade networks.
gsSubTopics: Indian Heritage and Culture, History and Geography of the World and Society.
Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.
prelimsPoints: New pre-Harappan settlement discovered in Gujarat.
Findings include pottery, tools, planned structures.
Sheds light on evolution of Indus Valley Civilization.
Location suggests role in early trade networks.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of new archaeological discoveries in reshaping our understanding of ancient Indian history and the Indus Valley Civilization.
Analyze the characteristics of pre-Harappan cultures and their contribution to the development of urban civilizations in the subcontinent.
Evaluate the methods and challenges involved in archaeological research in India.
Examine the potential implications of such discoveries for understanding trade, social structures, and cultural exchanges in ancient India.
GS1.imageDescription: Archaeologists at an excavation site, carefully unearthing artifacts.
GS1.trivia: The Indus Valley Civilization is one of the world's three early cradles of civilization, along with ancient Egypt and Mesopotamia.

GS1.headline: Urban Local Bodies Directed to Enhance Monsoon Preparedness Against Flooding
GS1.explainer:
• The Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs has issued directives to urban local bodies (ULBs) across the country to intensify monsoon preparedness measures.
• The focus is on clearing drainage systems, identifying flood-prone areas, upgrading storm water infrastructure, and developing early warning systems.
• Emphasizes community engagement and public awareness campaigns on flood safety and waste management to prevent blockages.
• The directives come in light of increasing instances of urban flooding due to erratic rainfall patterns and rapid urbanization.
• Calls for integrating climate-resilient urban planning into long-term development strategies.
gsSubTopics: Important Geophysical phenomena such as earthquakes, Tsunami, Volcanic activity, cyclone etc., geographical features and their location-changes in critical geographical features (including water-bodies and ice-caps) and in flora and fauna and the effects of such changes.
Urbanization, their problems and their remedies.
GS1.source: Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs
prelimsPoints: Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs directed ULBs for monsoon preparedness.
Focus on clearing drainage, identifying flood-prone areas, upgrading storm water infrastructure.
Emphasizes community engagement and public awareness.
Calls for integrating climate-resilient urban planning.
mainsPoints: Analyze the primary causes and increasing frequency of urban flooding in Indian cities.
Critically evaluate the existing disaster management frameworks and urban planning strategies in addressing urban flooding.
Discuss the socio-economic impacts of urban flooding on city dwellers and infrastructure.
Suggest comprehensive measures, including structural and non-structural interventions, for effective urban flood management and climate-resilient urban development.
GS1.imageDescription: A waterlogged street in an Indian city during heavy monsoon rain.
GS1.trivia: Mumbai's Mithi River, once a clean river, is now heavily polluted and prone to overflowing, contributing to the city's urban flooding challenges.

GS1.headline: Several Traditional Indian Crafts Awarded GI Tag, Boosting Artisanal Livelihoods
GS1.explainer:
• The Geographical Indications (GI) Registry has recently granted GI tags to several traditional Indian crafts from different states.
• This recognition helps protect the unique identity and quality of these indigenous products, preventing their unauthorized use.
• The move is expected to boost the livelihoods of local artisans, promote traditional knowledge, and enhance the market value of these crafts.
• Examples include a unique form of pottery from Rajasthan, handloom textiles from the North East, and a specific wood carving style from South India.
• The government aims to further promote these GI-tagged products through national and international exhibitions.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.
Salient features of Indian Society, Diversity of India.
GS1.source: Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT)
prelimsPoints: Several traditional Indian crafts received GI tags.
Protects unique identity and quality of indigenous products.
Aims to boost artisans' livelihoods and promote traditional knowledge.
Government plans to promote GI-tagged products nationally and internationally.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of Geographical Indication (GI) tags in preserving India's cultural heritage and promoting local economies.
Analyze how GI tags contribute to protecting traditional knowledge and preventing imitation of indigenous products.
Evaluate the challenges faced by traditional artisans in India and how policy interventions like GI tagging can address them.
Suggest further measures to enhance the market access and global recognition of India's diverse traditional crafts.
GS1.imageDescription: A collection of colorful traditional Indian handicrafts.
GS1.trivia: Darjeeling Tea was the first Indian product to receive a Geographical Indication (GI) tag in 2004-05.

GS2.headline: Centre Launches 'Gram Uday' Scheme for Sustainable Rural Transformation
GS2.explainer:
• The Union Government today unveiled the 'Gram Uday' scheme, a comprehensive initiative aimed at holistic rural development across the nation.
• The scheme integrates various existing programs under a single umbrella, focusing on sustainable livelihoods, climate resilience, and infrastructure development.
• Key pillars include promoting organic farming, water conservation, renewable energy adoption in villages, and enhancing digital literacy.
• Emphasizes community participation and Gram Panchayat empowerment for localized planning and implementation.
• Aims to bridge the rural-urban divide by improving access to education, healthcare, and economic opportunities in rural areas.
gsSubTopics: Government Policies and Interventions for Development in various sectors and Issues arising out of their Design and Implementation
Welfare Schemes for Vulnerable Sections of the population by the Centre and States and the Performance of these Schemes
Role of NGOs, SHGs, various groups and associations, donors, charities, institutional and other stakeholders
prelimsPoints: 'Gram Uday' scheme focuses on sustainable livelihoods, climate resilience, and infrastructure.
It integrates existing rural development programs.
Emphasizes Gram Panchayat empowerment and community participation.
Targets include organic farming, water conservation, renewable energy, and digital literacy in rural areas.
mainsPoints: Discuss the multi-faceted approach of Gram Uday in addressing rural challenges.
Evaluate the potential impact of integrating various schemes under one umbrella for better synergy and resource utilization.
Analyze the role of decentralized planning and community participation in achieving sustainable rural transformation.
Examine how the scheme aims to address climate resilience and promote sustainable agricultural practices.
GS2.imageDescription: Illustration depicting a vibrant Indian village scene with elements of renewable energy, organic farming, and digital literacy.
GS2.trivia: Mahatma Gandhi's vision of 'Gram Swaraj' emphasized self-sufficient and self-governing villages, a principle often echoed in India's rural development policies.

GS2.headline: Supreme Court to Review Compliance on Electoral Bonds Transparency
GS2.explainer:
• The Supreme Court has indicated it will review the compliance status of its earlier judgment regarding the disclosure of electoral bond details.
• This move follows petitions alleging incomplete or delayed disclosure by certain entities and political parties.
• The Court had previously struck down the Electoral Bond Scheme as unconstitutional, citing violations of the right to information and voter's right to know.
• The review aims to ensure full transparency in political funding ahead of upcoming state elections.
• The judgment has been a landmark decision in promoting financial transparency in Indian politics.
gsSubTopics: Indian Constitution—historical underpinnings, evolution, features, amendments, significant provisions and basic structure.
Separation of powers between various organs dispute redressal mechanisms and institutions.
Salient features of the Representation of People’s Act.
prelimsPoints: Supreme Court reviewing compliance on electoral bond disclosure.
Court previously struck down the scheme as unconstitutional.
Cited violation of right to information and voter's right to know.
Aims to ensure full transparency in political funding.
mainsPoints: Discuss the implications of the Supreme Court's ruling on electoral bonds for democratic transparency and accountability.
Analyze the arguments for and against electoral bonds in the context of political funding reforms.
Evaluate the role of the judiciary in upholding constitutional principles related to fundamental rights like the right to information.
Suggest alternative mechanisms for transparent political funding in India.
GS2.imageDescription: Image of the Supreme Court of India building.
GS2.trivia: The first general elections in India in 1951-52 saw the use of ballot boxes, a system that has evolved significantly over decades to electronic voting machines.

GS2.headline: India and Japan Conclude 'JIMEX 24' Naval Exercise, Bolstering Maritime Security Cooperation
GS2.explainer:
• The bilateral maritime exercise 'JIMEX 24' between the Indian Navy and the Japan Maritime Self-Defense Force (JMSDF) concluded successfully in the Arabian Sea.
• The exercise focused on enhancing interoperability, sharing best practices, and strengthening maritime security cooperation in the Indo-Pacific region.
• Drills included anti-submarine warfare, anti-air warfare, tactical maneuvers, and communication exercises.
• JIMEX (Japan India Maritime Exercise) is a biennial exercise reflecting the growing strategic partnership between the two nations.
• The collaboration underscores a shared vision for a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific, crucial for regional stability and economic prosperity.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighbourhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
prelimsPoints: JIMEX 24 is a bilateral naval exercise between India and Japan.
Concluded in the Arabian Sea.
Aimed at enhancing interoperability and maritime security in the Indo-Pacific.
Focuses on anti-submarine, anti-air warfare, and tactical maneuvers.
mainsPoints: Discuss the strategic significance of India-Japan defense cooperation in the context of Indo-Pacific security dynamics.
Analyze how joint naval exercises like JIMEX contribute to regional stability and counterbalancing emerging challenges.
Evaluate the role of bilateral and multilateral security partnerships in India's foreign policy objectives.
Examine the shared vision of India and Japan for a free, open, and inclusive Indo-Pacific and its implications.
GS2.imageDescription: Warships of the Indian and Japanese navies conducting maneuvers at sea.
GS2.trivia: The Indian Navy's oldest operational aircraft carrier, INS Vikrant (R11), served for over 36 years before being decommissioned.

GS2.headline: Mid-Term Review of National Health Policy 2017 Shows Mixed Progress, Focus on Primary Healthcare
GS2.explainer:
• A mid-term review of the National Health Policy 2017 revealed mixed progress in achieving its ambitious targets, particularly in public health spending.
• Significant strides have been made in expanding Ayushman Bharat-PMJAY coverage and improving maternal and child health indicators.
• Challenges persist in strengthening primary healthcare infrastructure, addressing human resource shortages, and ensuring equitable access in rural areas.
• The review highlighted the need for increased state government allocation to health and better coordination between central and state health programs.
• Emphasizes leveraging digital health technologies and promoting preventive healthcare for better outcomes.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
Welfare Schemes for Vulnerable Sections of the population by the Centre and States and the Performance of these Schemes.
GS2.source: Ministry of Health and Family Welfare
prelimsPoints: Mid-term review of National Health Policy 2017 shows mixed progress.
Progress in Ayushman Bharat-PMJAY and maternal/child health.
Challenges in primary healthcare infrastructure and human resources.
Need for increased state health allocation and better center-state coordination.
mainsPoints: Critically analyze the achievements and shortcomings of the National Health Policy 2017 in improving India's public health outcomes.
Discuss the challenges in achieving universal healthcare coverage and equitable access, especially in rural and remote areas.
Evaluate the role of flagship schemes like Ayushman Bharat-PMJAY in transforming India's healthcare landscape.
Suggest policy recommendations to strengthen primary healthcare and address human resource shortages in the health sector.
GS2.imageDescription: A doctor interacting with patients in a rural health center.
GS2.trivia: The 'Janani Shishu Suraksha Karyakram' (JSSK) was launched in 2011 to reduce maternal and infant mortality by providing free services to pregnant women and sick newborns.

GS2.headline: Experts Discuss Implementation Challenges of National Education Policy 2020
GS2.explainer:
• A national conference of educationists and policymakers discussed the ongoing challenges in the effective implementation of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020.
• Key issues highlighted include infrastructure gaps, teacher training and recruitment, curriculum development for multidisciplinary learning, and funding constraints.
• Emphasis was laid on the need for greater collaboration between central and state governments for seamless policy rollout.
• The NEP 2020 aims to transform India's education system from early childhood to higher education, focusing on holistic development and skill building.
• Stakeholders called for a phased and adaptive approach, prioritizing foundational literacy and numeracy.
gsSubTopics: Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources.
Government Policies and Interventions for Development in various sectors and Issues arising out of their Design and Implementation.
prelimsPoints: Conference discussed NEP 2020 implementation challenges.
Issues: infrastructure, teacher training, curriculum, funding.
Need for center-state collaboration for rollout.
NEP aims for holistic development and skill building.
mainsPoints: Analyze the transformative potential of the National Education Policy 2020 in reforming India's education system.
Discuss the major implementation challenges faced by the NEP 2020, particularly concerning resources, human capital, and federal cooperation.
Evaluate the importance of foundational literacy and numeracy, and multidisciplinary education as envisioned by the NEP.
Suggest strategies for effective and equitable implementation of the NEP across diverse educational landscapes in India.
GS2.imageDescription: Students engaged in learning activities in a modern classroom setting.
GS2.trivia: India's first National Policy on Education was formulated in 1968, followed by significant revisions in 1986 and 1992.

GS2.headline: India-Africa Business Summit Concludes, Pledges Enhanced Trade and Investment
GS2.explainer:
• The India-Africa Business Summit concluded in New Delhi, bringing together business leaders and policymakers from both regions.
• The summit focused on enhancing bilateral trade, fostering investment, and promoting technology transfer in key sectors like agriculture, healthcare, and digital economy.
• India reiterated its commitment to capacity building and development partnerships with African nations, aligning with the 'SAGAR' vision.
• Several Memoranda of Understanding (MoUs) were signed to facilitate joint ventures and infrastructure projects.
• The event underscored the growing strategic importance of India-Africa relations in a multipolar world.
gsSubTopics: India and its neighbourhood- relations.
Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
prelimsPoints: India-Africa Business Summit concluded in New Delhi.
Focused on enhancing bilateral trade, investment, technology transfer.
India committed to capacity building and development partnerships.
Several MoUs signed for joint ventures and infrastructure projects.
mainsPoints: Discuss the evolving dynamics and strategic significance of India-Africa relations in the 21st century.
Analyze the key pillars of India's engagement with African nations, including trade, investment, and development cooperation.
Evaluate the challenges and opportunities in strengthening economic ties between India and Africa.
Examine how India's 'SAGAR' vision and South-South cooperation principles guide its foreign policy towards Africa.
GS2.imageDescription: Business leaders from India and African nations shaking hands at a conference.
GS2.trivia: The 'Bandung Conference' in 1955 was a significant milestone in Afro-Asian solidarity, laying the groundwork for the Non-Aligned Movement.

GS3.headline: India Reiterates Call for Enhanced Climate Finance at COP29 Preparatory Talks
GS3.explainer:
• India's delegation at the preparatory talks for COP29 emphasized the critical need for developed nations to meet their climate finance commitments.
• Stressed the importance of predictable and adequate financial support for developing countries to achieve their climate mitigation and adaptation goals.
• Highlighted the principle of 'Common but Differentiated Responsibilities and Respective Capabilities' (CBDR-RC) as foundational to global climate action.
• Called for a clear roadmap for achieving the $100 billion annual climate finance goal and scaling up beyond it post-2025.
• India presented its progress in renewable energy deployment and sustainable development initiatives, urging global collaboration.
gsSubTopics: Environmental pollution and degradation
Conservation
Environmental impact assessment
India and its neighbourhood- relations.
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora.
prelimsPoints: India emphasized enhanced climate finance at COP29 preparatory talks.
Called for predictable and adequate financial support for developing nations.
Reiterated CBDR-RC principle.
Demanded clear roadmap for $100 billion climate finance goal and beyond.
mainsPoints: Discuss India's consistent stance on climate finance at international forums and its rationale.
Analyze the concept of 'Common but Differentiated Responsibilities and Respective Capabilities' (CBDR-RC) in the context of global climate justice.
Evaluate the challenges in achieving climate finance goals and the implications for developing countries like India.
Propose strategies for effective global collaboration to address climate change, considering equity and historical responsibility.
GS3.imageDescription: Delegates from various countries at a climate conference.
GS3.trivia: The first Earth Summit in Rio de Janeiro in 1992 led to the establishment of the UNFCCC, the framework for international climate action.

GS3.headline: Air Quality Deteriorates in Northern Indian Cities; Government Urges Stricter Enforcement
GS3.explainer:
• A recent report indicates a significant deterioration in air quality across several northern Indian cities, including Delhi-NCR, with AQI levels entering the 'very poor' category.
• Factors contributing to the worsening air quality include stubble burning, vehicular emissions, industrial pollution, and unfavorable meteorological conditions.
• The government has urged state authorities to implement stricter measures under the Graded Response Action Plan (GRAP) and ensure public compliance.
• Health advisories have been issued, particularly for vulnerable populations, recommending reduced outdoor activities.
• Long-term strategies focusing on renewable energy, public transport, and industrial emission controls are being re-evaluated for faster implementation.
gsSubTopics: Environmental pollution and degradation
Conservation
Environmental impact assessment
prelimsPoints: Air quality in northern Indian cities worsened to 'very poor' category.
Causes include stubble burning, vehicular emissions, industrial pollution.
Government urged stricter GRAP implementation.
Health advisories issued for vulnerable groups.
mainsPoints: Analyze the multi-faceted causes of air pollution in northern India and its seasonal variations.
Critically evaluate the effectiveness of existing policies and action plans like GRAP in mitigating air pollution.
Discuss the socio-economic and health impacts of deteriorating air quality on urban populations.
Suggest comprehensive long-term and short-term strategies to effectively combat air pollution in India.
GS3.imageDescription: Smog-filled skyline of a major Indian city.
GS3.trivia: The Great Smog of London in 1952 led to significant changes in environmental regulations and cleaner air acts in the UK.

GS3.headline: ISRO Successfully Launches Next-Gen Weather Satellite 'Kalpana-3'
GS3.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) successfully launched 'Kalpana-3', an advanced geostationary meteorological satellite, from Satish Dhawan Space Centre.
• Kalpana-3 is designed to provide high-resolution images of weather patterns, monitor ocean surface temperatures, and track atmospheric phenomena.
• Equipped with advanced payloads including a multi-spectral imager and a sounder, it will significantly enhance India's weather forecasting capabilities.
• The satellite is a successor to the Kalpana series, named in honor of astronaut Kalpana Chawla, and will bolster disaster warning systems.
• This mission reinforces India's growing self-reliance and leadership in space technology for societal applications.
gsSubTopics: Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology.
Awareness in the fields of Space.
Disaster and disaster management.
prelimsPoints: ISRO launched 'Kalpana-3', a next-gen geostationary meteorological satellite.
Designed for high-resolution weather imaging, ocean temperature monitoring, atmospheric tracking.
Equipped with multi-spectral imager and sounder.
Successor to Kalpana series, named after Kalpana Chawla.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of advanced meteorological satellites like Kalpana-3 for India's weather forecasting and disaster management capabilities.
Analyze ISRO's contributions to societal applications through its space missions, citing relevant examples.
Evaluate India's growing self-reliance and leadership in space technology and its implications for national development.
Examine the role of space-based observations in understanding and mitigating the impacts of climate change.
GS3.imageDescription: Rocket launching into space with 'Kalpana-3' satellite.
GS3.trivia: Kalpana Chawla was the first woman of Indian origin to go to space, flying on the Space Shuttle Columbia in 1997.

GS3.headline: Government Unveils New Incentives to Boost MSME Exports and Global Competitiveness
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Micro, Small & Medium Enterprises (MSME) announced a new package of incentives and support mechanisms to significantly boost MSME exports.
• Measures include enhanced credit access for export-oriented units, simplified customs procedures, and subsidies for international trade fair participation.
• Focus is on developing specialized export clusters and promoting 'Made in India' brands in global markets.
• A new digital portal has been launched to streamline export documentation and provide market intelligence to MSMEs.
• Aims to integrate Indian MSMEs more deeply into global value chains and achieve higher export growth targets.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization, of resources, growth, development and employment.
Inclusive growth and issues arising from it.
Effects of liberalization on the economy, changes in industrial policy and their effects on industrial growth.
prelimsPoints: Ministry of MSME launched new incentives for MSME exports.
Measures include enhanced credit, simplified customs, trade fair subsidies.
Focus on export clusters and 'Made in India' brands.
New digital portal for export documentation and market intelligence.
mainsPoints: Discuss the significance of MSMEs in India's economic growth and employment generation.
Analyze the challenges faced by Indian MSMEs in accessing global markets and competing internationally.
Evaluate the effectiveness of government initiatives aimed at boosting MSME exports and integrating them into global value chains.
Suggest further policy interventions to enhance the global competitiveness of India's MSME sector.
GS3.imageDescription: MSME products being prepared for export with a globe in the background.
GS3.trivia: India's MSME sector contributes significantly to the country's GDP and employment, often referred to as the 'backbone of the economy'.

GS3.headline: RBI Keeps Repo Rate Unchanged Amidst Persistent Inflationary Concerns
GS3.explainer:
• The Reserve Bank of India (RBI) in its latest Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) meeting decided to keep the benchmark repo rate unchanged at 6.5%.
• The decision reflects a cautious stance, prioritizing inflation control despite signs of moderating economic growth.
• RBI Governor highlighted concerns over volatile food prices and global crude oil fluctuations as key inflationary risks.
• The MPC reiterated its commitment to aligning inflation with the target while supporting growth.
• Future policy decisions will remain data-dependent, closely monitoring domestic and international economic indicators.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization, of resources, growth, development and employment.
Government Budgeting.
Monetary policy.
prelimsPoints: RBI kept repo rate unchanged at 6.5%.
Decision driven by persistent inflationary concerns.
Volatile food prices and crude oil cited as risks.
MPC committed to inflation target and growth support.
mainsPoints: Discuss the primary objectives of the RBI's monetary policy and the tools it employs to achieve them.
Analyze the current economic challenges facing India, particularly the trade-off between inflation control and economic growth.
Evaluate the rationale behind the RBI's decision to maintain the repo rate and its potential impact on various sectors of the economy.
Examine the role of the Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) in India's economic governance and its independence.
GS3.imageDescription: RBI building facade with the logo.
GS3.trivia: The Reserve Bank of India was established on April 1, 1935, in accordance with the Reserve Bank of India Act, 1934.

GS3.headline: India Unveils Enhanced Cybersecurity Framework to Combat Rising Digital Threats
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) has released an enhanced National Cybersecurity Framework, updating existing policies.
• The new framework focuses on critical infrastructure protection, promoting indigenous cybersecurity solutions, and fostering international cooperation.
• Introduces stricter regulations for data protection and incident response protocols for government agencies and private entities.
• Aims to build a robust cyber defense ecosystem, including skill development and public awareness campaigns against cybercrime.
• This move comes in response to the growing sophistication of cyberattacks targeting both public and private sectors.
gsSubTopics: Challenges to internal security through communication networks, role of media and social networking sites in internal security challenges, basics of cyber security; money-laundering and its prevention.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
GS3.source: Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY)
prelimsPoints: MeitY unveiled enhanced National Cybersecurity Framework.
Focuses on critical infrastructure protection, indigenous solutions, international cooperation.
Stricter regulations for data protection and incident response.
Aims to build robust cyber defense ecosystem and skill development.
mainsPoints: Discuss the evolving landscape of cyber threats faced by India and their potential impact on national security and economic stability.
Analyze the key provisions and objectives of India's enhanced National Cybersecurity Framework.
Evaluate the challenges in implementing a comprehensive cybersecurity strategy, including technological, human resource, and legal aspects.
Suggest measures for fostering a culture of cybersecurity awareness and promoting indigenous capabilities in this domain.
GS3.imageDescription: Abstract image representing digital security and a network grid.
GS3.trivia: The first computer virus, 'Creeper', was created in 1971, while the first known cyberattack on critical infrastructure was the Stuxnet worm in 2010.

GS3.headline: India Accelerates Renewable Energy Deployment to Meet Ambitious 2030 Targets
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of New and Renewable Energy announced accelerated plans to deploy renewable energy projects, aiming to surpass the 500 GW target by 2030.
• Focus includes large-scale solar parks, offshore wind farms, and green hydrogen initiatives.
• New policies offer incentives for private sector investment and technological innovation in the renewable energy sector.
• Emphasizes the role of renewable energy in achieving energy security, reducing carbon emissions, and fostering green jobs.
• International collaborations and technology transfer are being actively pursued to scale up deployment.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life.
GS3.source: Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE)
prelimsPoints: MNRE announced accelerated renewable energy deployment plans.
Aims to surpass 500 GW target by 2030.
Focus on solar parks, offshore wind, green hydrogen.
New policies for private investment and tech innovation.
mainsPoints: Discuss India's ambitious renewable energy targets and their significance for climate action and energy security.
Analyze the challenges and opportunities in scaling up renewable energy deployment in India, including land acquisition, grid integration, and financing.
Evaluate the role of government policies and private sector investment in driving India's energy transition.
Examine the potential of green hydrogen as a future energy source for India and the steps being taken for its development.
GS3.imageDescription: A vast solar power farm stretching into the horizon.
GS3.trivia: India is home to the world's largest solar power plant, Bhadla Solar Park in Rajasthan, with a capacity of over 2.2 GW.

sources: Ministry of Health and Family Welfare
Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs
Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY)
Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE)
